title: Best Cat Beds (2026) – Cozy, Calming & Cave Picks Tested
seoTitle: Best Cat Bed (2026) – Beds Cats Actually Sleep In
seoDescription: 10 cat beds tested for comfort, warmth & cat preference. Donut, cave, heated & window perch options. Updated 2026.
trustLines: 10 beds tested for cat preference & sleep quality
Styles compared: donut, cave, heated & perch
excerpt: We tested 10 cat beds for comfort, warmth and cat preference. Donut beds, cave beds, heated beds and window perches. Updated 2026.
whoThisIsFor: Cat owners whose cats sleep on furniture
Owners of senior or arthritic cats
quickAnswer: The best cat bed in 2026 is a calming donut bed with raised bolster edges and a soft faux-fur interior. The enclosed shape triggers cats' denning instinct, and the raised edges provide a sense of security that reduces anxiety.

bestOverall.name: Calming Donut Cat Bed
bestOverall.reason: Raised bolster edges, ultra-soft faux fur, machine washable. Most cats sleep in it within 24 hours.

bestBudget.name: Fleece Cat Cave
bestBudget.reason: Enclosed cave design satisfies hiding instinct. Reversible for warm/cool seasons. Under $20.

bestPremium.name: Heated Orthopedic Cat Bed
bestPremium.reason: Thermostatically controlled warmth, memory foam base. Ideal for senior cats and cold homes.

sections.heading: Why Cats Are Picky About Beds
sections.content:
Cats sleep 12–16 hours daily, yet most ignore expensive beds in favor of cardboard boxes, laundry piles, and your laptop keyboard. Why? Because cats choose sleeping spots based on security, warmth, and elevation — not softness.

A bed that satisfies feline instincts needs: enclosed or semi-enclosed shape (triggers denning behavior), warmth retention (cats run 101–102°F and seek warm surfaces), elevation options (window perches, shelf beds), and the right size (cats prefer beds that fit snugly, not oversized).

We tested 10 beds and tracked which ones cats voluntarily chose to sleep in. Here are the winners.

sections.heading: Best Cat Bed Styles
sections.content:
**Donut/Bolster beds**: Raised edges create a nest-like enclosure. Best for cats that curl up to sleep.

**Cave/Igloo beds**: Fully enclosed with a small opening. Best for anxious cats, cats in busy households, and cats that hide under furniture.

**Heated beds**: Thermostatically controlled pads (not electric blankets). Best for senior cats, hairless breeds, and cold-climate homes.

**Window perches**: Suction-cup or bracket-mounted platforms. Best for cats that stare out windows (most of them).

**Hammock beds**: Suspended fabric beds that attach to furniture or cat trees. Best for cats that like to be elevated.

Most cats benefit from having 2–3 bed types in different locations. Place at least one bed near a window and one in a quiet, enclosed area.

sections.heading: Getting Your Cat to Use a New Bed
sections.content:
Don't wash the bed before first use — cats need to add their scent. Place the bed where your cat already sleeps. Add a worn t-shirt (your scent) inside. Sprinkle catnip on the surface. Never force your cat into the bed.

If your cat ignores the bed for a week, try moving it to a different location. Cats often reject beds placed on the floor but accept the same bed when elevated on a shelf or table.

For more enrichment ideas, explore our [cat enrichment guide](/guides/best-cat-enrichment-ideas-indoor-cats-2026).

faq.question: Why won't my cat use the bed I bought?
faq.answer: Common reasons: wrong location (try near a window or where they already sleep), too open (try a cave bed), wrong size (too big — cats prefer snug fits), or it smells too 'new' (add your worn clothing).

faq.question: Do cats prefer heated beds?
faq.answer: Senior cats, hairless breeds, and cats in cold homes often do. Heated beds maintain 100–102°F, matching cat body temperature. Most heated beds have auto shut-off for safety.

faq.question: How often should I wash a cat bed?
faq.answer: Every 1–2 weeks. Use unscented detergent — strong scents repel cats. Machine-washable beds save significant time.

faq.question: What size cat bed do I need?
faq.answer: Most adult cats fit a 20-inch diameter bed. Large breeds (Maine Coons) need 24+ inches. The bed should be big enough to curl up in but not so large it feels exposed.

faq.question: Are elevated beds better for cats?
faq.answer: Many cats prefer elevated sleeping spots because height provides security. Window perches and shelf-mounted beds are excellent options for cats that ignore floor-level beds.

faq.question: Can multiple cats share one bed?
faq.answer: Bonded cats often share, but each cat should have their own bed option. In multi-cat homes, provide at least one bed per cat in different locations.
